one_line_takeaway: DistractMIA is an output-only black-box VLM membership-inference attack that inserts a calibrated semantic 'distractor' patch into an image and scores membership by how much the model's generated description shifts toward the distractor versus staying anchored to the original image, outperforming both prior output-only (KCMP) and stronger probability-access baselines across natural-image and medical VLM benchmarks.
key_finding: On VL-MIA/Flickr, DistractMIA's average AUROC across the three target VLMs rises from 0.675 (K=1) to 0.916 (K=10) to 0.989 (K=30), compared to the strongest output-only baseline KCMP at 0.526/0.556/0.594 (K=1/10/30) and Image Infer (Rouge) at 0.516/0.536/0.556; DistractMIA also beats every stronger-access (probability-based) baseline, including Perplexity (0.629/0.845/0.942). On VL-MIA/DALL-E (harder, semantically paired non-members), DistractMIA's average AUROC is 0.551/0.668/0.753 at K=1/10/30, again the best of all compared methods, though absolute numbers are lower than on Flickr. On the medical PMC benchmark with LLaVA-Med, DistractMIA reaches 0.692/0.954/0.999 AUROC at K=1/10/30 versus KCMP which the paper reports could not be meaningfully applied ('-' entries in Table 3) because clinically relevant regions in medical images lack clear object boundaries for mask construction; other baselines plateau around 0.5-0.6. The attention-shift mechanism analysis found non-member samples exhibit a larger mean relative attention shift toward the inserted distractor region (56.3%) than member samples (48.0%), and the gap widens further in the tail of the shift distribution (P(delta_AMR>t) diverges as t increases), offered as a mechanistic explanation for why member responses stay more stable under distraction. Sensitivity analysis shows performance improves as the stability weight eta increases from 0.3 to 0.7 (then slightly drops at 0.9), and improves monotonically as the number of stochastic generations T increases from 3 to 7.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is DistractMIA, an output-only black-box membership inference attack (MIA) against vision-language models (VLMs) that infers training-data membership purely from generated text (no logits, probabilities, or hidden states). Instead of masking image regions (as prior work KCMP does), DistractMIA preserves the original image and inserts a known 'semantic distractor' patch, then measures how the model's generated description changes. The method has two stages: (1) a calibration stage that uses a small disjoint reference set (COCO, 50 member + 50 non-member images) and a REINFORCE-trained policy over discrete distractor configurations (location, semantic pattern, size) to select a distractor configuration P* that maximizes a reward balancing member/non-member separability across two stratified reference splits while penalizing inconsistency between splits; (2) an inference stage that, with P* frozen, queries the target VLM T=5 times each on the original and distracted image, computes a response-stability score S(z) (average pairwise Jaccard similarity of normalized word sets across generations) and a distractor-mention rate M(z), combines them into G(z)=eta*S(z)-(1-eta)*M(z) with eta=0.7, and outputs the membership score A(x)=G(x_distracted)-G(x_original). Larger A(x) indicates the model stayed anchored to the original image (evidence of membership); the intuition is that memorized (member) images make the model's description more resistant to the inserted distractor, while non-member images are more easily redirected toward it.
threat_model: Calibrated output-only black-box setting. The adversary/auditor can query a deployed target VLM with an image and prompt and observe only the generated text response -- no access to model parameters, gradients, logits, or token-level probabilities/hidden representations. The adversary is assumed to additionally have access to a small public reference set (e.g., COCO) with known member/non-member labels relative to the target model's training data, used only for offline calibration of the distractor configuration; this reference set is disjoint from the evaluation set. The goal is training-data auditing: given an image x, determine whether x was part of the target VLM's training corpus, motivated by privacy/copyright auditing of deployed VLM services (private photos, medical images, copyrighted or user-uploaded content).
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations (Conclusion section): DistractMIA depends on effective distractor selection and repeated generations, which increases calibration and query cost (T=5 queries per original and distracted image per audited sample, plus the RL-based calibration search over a reference set). Evaluation is confined to single-turn image-description settings; extending semantic distraction to multi-turn or reasoning-intensive VLM tasks is left as future work. Additionally noted in the body: the method assumes access to a small, disjoint, labeled reference set (public dataset such as COCO) for calibration, which may not always be available or representative of the true training distribution; absolute AUROC on the harder VL-MIA/DALL-E benchmark remains modest (0.551-0.753) at low K, well below the near-ceiling Flickr and medical results, indicating the attack's advantage shrinks considerably when temporal/distributional shortcuts between members and non-members are removed.

datasets_used: VL-MIA/Flickr (600 images: 300 member from MS COCO Flickr subset used by target models, 300 non-member from photos uploaded after Jan 1, 2024; temporal split; includes corrupted member-image variants per official VL-MIA setup)

VL-MIA/DALL-E (592 images; members from intersection of LAION-CCS training images [LAION, Conceptual Captions 3M/12M, SBU]; non-members are DALL-E-generated images semantically paired one-to-one with members)

PMC-OA / PMC medical benchmark (used with LLaVA-Med; reference and evaluation subsets are strictly non-overlapping splits of the same source dataset since LLaVA-Med's exact training data is not fully available)
COCO reference/calibration set (100 images: 50 member + 50 non-member, split 60/40 into two disjoint stratified splits R(1)/R(2) for the RL-based distractor search; disjoint from evaluation sets)
quantitative_results: VL-MIA/Flickr average AUROC (K=1/10/30): DistractMIA 0.675/0.916/0.989 vs. KCMP 0.526/0.556/0.594 vs. Image Infer (Rouge) 0.516/0.536/0.556 vs. Perplexity 0.629/0.845/0.942 (strongest probability-access baseline)
VL-MIA/DALL-E average AUROC (K=1/10/30): DistractMIA 0.551/0.668/0.753 vs. KCMP 0.507/0.535/0.542 vs. Perplexity 0.530/0.635/0.708

LLaVA-Med / PMC medical benchmark AUROC (K=1/10/30): DistractMIA 0.692/0.954/0.999 vs. Perplexity 0.523/0.524/0.556 vs. Min-K% 0.498/0.517/0.607 vs. MaxRenyi-K% 0.545/0.565/0.604 vs. Image Infer (Rouge) 0.486/0.512/0.428; KCMP not reported ('-') because mask-based probes are difficult to construct on low-semantic-density medical images
Attention Mass Ratio (AMR) mean relative shift under distraction: members 48.0% vs. non-members 56.3%, with the gap widening in the tail P(delta_AMR>t) as t increases

Sensitivity: performance rises as stability weight eta increases from 0.3 to 0.7 then slightly drops at eta=0.9; performance rises monotonically as generation count T increases from 3 to 7, with DistractMIA remaining above KCMP and ModRenyi across all tested T
baselines_compared: KCMP (masked semantic prediction, output-only, most closely related prior black-box VLM MIA)
Image Infer (Rouge and MPNet variants; caption-similarity across repeated queries, output-only)
